title: Spinning art yarn: thick-thin singles
subtitle: Deliberately varying the draft zone to produce an irregular, textured singles yarn
excerpt: Art yarn, at its simplest, is a singles spun with intentional variation in thickness. A thick-thin singles has sections of very fine yarn alternating with sections of very thick, lofty roving held together by minimal twist. The technique reverses the usual goal of even spinning and instead controls the irregularity. The resulting yarn is used for weaving accents, textile art, and decorative crochet or knitting.
sourceNotes: Art yarn as a textile art form emerged in the late 20th century. Thick-thin singles are among the most straightforward art yarn structures. Method is observation-led.

glossaryTerms.definition: The section of roving between the front fingers and the back fingers during spinning, where fibres are pulled apart before receiving twist. Controlling the length and position of the draft zone determines yarn thickness.
An art yarn structure in which very fine spun sections alternate with very thick, lightly twisted sections. The contrast in thickness is deliberate and part of the finished yarn's character.

content.text: singles is deliberately unstable. The spinner alternates between a long, open draft (producing a fine section) and a short, closed draft with almost no fibre separation (producing a thick section). Both sections receive the same amount of twist, so the thick sections are underspun relative to the fine ones. The underspun sections need enough twist to hold together but no more.

attrs.title: Wheel safety
attrs.body: Keep hair, loose clothing, and cord away from the drive band and flyer. Tie back hair before starting.

items.name: Bulky or textured roving (BFL, Corriedale, or a prepared art fibre batt)
Spinning wheel set to a large orifice and low-ratio pulley

content.text: Step 1: Set the wheel for art yarn

Use the lowest drive-band ratio available on the wheel (the large whorl, small pulley setting). Low ratio means fewer flyer rotations per treadle, which means less twist per length. This prevents the thick sections from receiving so much twist that they harden. The orifice must be large enough to pass the thick sections through easily; if the wheel has a jumbo flyer, use it.

Treadle at a steady pace and draw the roving out to a thin strand for 10-15 cm, allowing twist to run in. This is the fine section. Aim for a consistent thickness across all fine sections; they provide the visual rhythm of the yarn.

To produce a thick section, stop drafting and hold a short length of undrafted roving between your hands without separating the fibres. Slow your treadling. Allow only 3-4 treadle strokes' worth of twist to enter this section before allowing it to feed onto the bobbin. The thick section should feel lofty and slightly under-twisted compared to the fine sections.

Art yarn thick-thin singles are typically used as-is rather than plied. Wind off onto the niddy-noddy, soak in lukewarm water for 10 minutes, and snap lightly to set the twist. Hang to dry without weights or stretching: the thick-thin texture is part of the yarn and should not be pulled out.

items.problem: The thick sections break when passing through the orifice
items.cause: Orifice is too small or thick sections are too large for the wheel's flyer
items.fix: Use a jumbo flyer, or manually reduce thick section size. If the wheel cannot pass the thick sections, the art yarn design needs to match the wheel.

items.problem: Thick sections are as over-twisted as fine sections
items.cause: Treadling speed not reduced during thick sections
items.fix: Slow treadle speed to 2-3 strokes per thick section. The twist must be minimal in thick sections.

items.problem: The contrast between thick and thin sections is barely visible
items.cause: Fine sections not drafting out enough, or thick sections not keeping enough mass
items.fix: Exaggerate both. The fine sections should be almost lace-weight thin; the thick sections should be bulky to super-bulky.
